steps.title: What Is Space?
steps.text:
## Space — defined precisely

**Space** is the **count of squares** behind your pawn line that your pieces can use freely. The deeper your pawns advance into enemy territory, the more space you control. Your **own pieces** then have more room to manoeuvre and **the enemy's pieces** have less.

### A simple test

Draw a line at the **middle of the board**. The side whose pawns sit **further forward** controls more space. In the position shown, White has pushed both **d4** and **e4**; Black's pawn sits on e5 only. White has the larger zone to play in.

### Two visual examples in prose

- *Maroczy Bind*: White pawns on **c4** and **e4** clamp Black's d-pawn forever. Black's minor pieces have only the squares **behind his own pawns**, while White rotates pieces between the wings at will.
- *King's Indian*: Black plays ...e5 and locks the centre with d6/e5. White's c4-d5 chain pushes Black backward, giving White a queenside space advantage. Black accepts cramped pieces in exchange for the kingside attack he plans later.

### How to USE space in your games

1. **Push pawns** to the 4th and 5th rank when they are **safely supported**.
2. **Keep pieces on the board** — your space cramps the opponent's pieces; if he trades, his pieces breathe again.
3. **Manoeuvre** in your space. Knights especially love big, open quadrants.
4. **Combine** space with weak squares — the squares the opponent gave up by retreating become outposts.

### What signs tell you you have space

- Your pawns sit on the **4th rank or beyond** while his are on the 3rd.
- Your pieces have **multiple good squares** to choose from; his have one or two.
- The opponent's pieces are **doubly placed** behind their own pawns.

### Common mistakes

- **Overextending** — too much space without piece support invites breaks (...c5, ...f5) that destroy the centre.
- **Trading carelessly** when you have space — every trade gives the cramped side relief.
- **Hesitating to push** when the opponent cannot react — space is *won by pushes*, not by hopes.

> Space is the by-product of pushing pawns when your pieces are ready.

steps.title: Exploiting Space
steps.text:
## Converting space into something concrete

A space advantage is **not a goal in itself** — it is a **resource**. The plan is always to convert space into a **second advantage**: weak squares, pawn breaks, an attack, an outpost, or a winning endgame.

### Capablanca's two rules

1. **The cramped side should trade pieces**. Less material, more room per piece.
2. **The side with space should AVOID trades**. Keep his pieces stuffed onto few squares.

These rules are not optional. Almost every space-vs-cramped game is decided by whether each side respects them.

### How to USE the space

1. **Press slowly**. Build threats on multiple files; the cramped side cannot defend them all.
2. **Re-route pieces** between wings. The cramped opponent re-routes more slowly than you do.
3. **Plan a pawn break** that turns space into an attack: f4-f5, h4-h5, c4-c5 are typical.
4. **Double on a file** the opponent cannot contest because his rooks are stuck behind cramped pieces.

### Two visual examples in prose

- *King's Indian, White side*: White has space on the queenside. He plays **a3, Rb1, b4, c5** — the slow build-up the cramped side cannot match. By the time Black has organised, the queenside is collapsing.
- *French Advance*: White owns the **e5/d4** wedge. His plan: build the queenside (Be3, Qd2, Rb1, b4-b5) while Black struggles to find squares for his bishop and knights.

### What signs tell you to apply it

- Your pawns are on the **4th-5th rank**, his on the **2nd-3rd**.
- He has a **bad bishop** stuck behind his own pawns.
- His pieces step on each other — knights blocking bishops, rooks blocking queens.

### Common mistakes

- **Trading queens** when you have space — the attacking potential of space evaporates.
- **Closing the position** further with your own pawns — your pieces cannot attack walls.
- Forgetting that **space is bought with risk** — extended pawns can become weak in an endgame.

> Squeeze the position with patience; the cramped side suffocates.
